title: Security and Compliance
content: This document captures security governance artifacts and compliance mappings for the
Actions platform (AMPS). These are reference materials for auditors and security reviewers,
not product features — they document the security posture rather than define implementable
code.
## Threat Model
The Actions platform maintains a lightweight threat model covering primary threats,
attack surfaces, and trust boundaries. The threat model is a living document, updated
when new attack surfaces are introduced.
### Primary Threats
| Threat | Category | Primary Controls |
| Unauthorized state transition | Workflow integrity | Guard condition enforcement, audit trail |
| Evidence tampering | Data integrity | Append-only audit log, hash verification |
| Cross-tenant data access | Multi-tenancy | PostgreSQL RLS, JWT `tenant_id` validation |
| Audit trail manipulation | Compliance | Append-only constraints, ledger verification |
| Unauthorized force transition | Authorization | Admin role enforcement, audit logging |
| CSRF on state mutations | Web security | BFF CSRF triple-layer protection |
### Attack Surface
| Surface | Exposure | Authentication | Key Controls |
| Actions API (`/api/v1/*`) | Authenticated | JWT Bearer / API Key | Authorization policies, RLS, audit logging |
| Admin API (`/api/v1/admin/*`) | Authenticated + Admin | JWT Bearer | Admin role check, RLS, audit logging |
| BFF proxy | Cookie-authenticated | Session cookie | CSRF triple-layer, proxy header hardening, CORS |
| Evidence upload | Authenticated | JWT Bearer | OWASP file validation (magic bytes, type whitelist, size limits) |
### Trust Boundaries
Browser ──[TLS + __Host- cookie + CSRF]──> Actions BFF
Actions BFF ──[JWT Bearer, stripped headers]──> Actions API
Actions API ──[RLS via SET LOCAL, parameterized queries]──> PostgreSQL
Actions API ──[TLS]──> Redis (sessions, cache)
Actions API ──[TLS, JWKS]──> Identity (JWT key refresh, API key validation)
Actions API ──[TLS]──> Azure Blob Storage (evidence files)
### Completeness Criteria
- Identifies at minimum: unauthorized state transitions, evidence tampering, cross-tenant access, audit trail manipulation, unauthorized force transitions, and CSRF as primary threats
- Each threat maps to one or more implementing controls
- Attack surface covers: Actions API (authenticated), Admin API (admin-only), BFF proxy layer, and evidence upload
- Trust boundary diagram documents security controls at each boundary
### CSRF Protection (BFF)
The BFF enforces three complementary CSRF defences. All three must pass — any
single failure rejects the request with 403.
| Layer | Mechanism | What It Prevents |
| `__Host-` cookie prefix | Requires `Secure`, `Path=/`, no `Domain` attribute | Subdomain cookie injection attacks |
| `SameSite=Strict` | Cookie is never sent on cross-origin requests | Classic CSRF (cross-origin form submissions, link clicks) |

| Custom header check | BFF requires `X-CSRF: 1` header on state-changing requests | Requests that bypass SameSite (e.g., top-level navigations in some browsers). Custom headers cannot be set cross-origin without CORS preflight, which the BFF does not allow. |
This is the same CSRF model used by the NxGN Identity BFF.
## Compliance Control Mapping
An explicit mapping from SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and ISO 45001 controls to implementing
FRs/NFRs across the Actions platform. Enables auditors (P3) to produce compliance
evidence without reading every PRD.
### SOC 2 Type II — Key Mappings
| Trust Services Criteria | Control | Implementing FR/NFR |
| CC6.1 Logical access | Authentication before access | JWT Bearer / API Key validation, BFF session management |
| CC6.2 Access provisioning | Role-based access | Admin role enforcement, workflow-scoped permissions |
| CC6.3 Access removal | Tenant isolation | RLS policies, JWT `tenant_id` claim enforcement |
| CC6.6 Threat management | Threat model, security testing | Threat Model (this document) |
| CC6.7 Transmission security | TLS, token encryption | QA-TLS ([Quality Attributes](quality-attributes.md)) |
| CC7.1 Infrastructure monitoring | Health checks, observability | QA-HEALTH-CHECKS, QA-OTEL-METRICS ([Quality Attributes](quality-attributes.md)) |
| CC7.2 Anomaly detection | Alerting thresholds | QA-ALERTING-THRESHOLDS ([Quality Attributes](quality-attributes.md)) |
| CC7.3 Change evaluation | Audit logging | Append-only audit trail per action, ledger verification |
| CC7.4 Incident management | Breach notification | Breach Notification (this document) |
| CC8.1 Change management | Change tracking | Audit logging, EF Core migrations, append-only audit trail with database-level immutability |
### GDPR — Key Mappings
| Article | Requirement | Implementing FR/NFR |
| Article 6 | Lawful basis for processing | Legitimate interest for safety/compliance action tracking |

| Article 17 | Right to erasure | Soft-delete pattern; regulatory retention (ISO 45001, MSHA, OSHA, EPA) overrides erasure per Article 17(3)(b) legal obligation exemption. See QA-DATA-LIFECYCLE ([Quality Attributes](quality-attributes.md)) |
| Article 20 | Data portability | Action data export capabilities |
| Article 25 | Data protection by design | RLS tenant isolation, PII redaction in logs (QA-OTEL-PII-REDACTION), encryption at rest |

| Article 32 | Security of processing | QA-TLS ([Quality Attributes](quality-attributes.md)), OWASP-compliant file validation (magic bytes, content-type verification, file type whitelist, 50MB size limit). Virus scanning deferred to post-Phase 1. |
| Article 33 | Breach notification to authority | Breach Notification (this document) |
| Article 34 | Breach notification to data subjects | Breach Notification (this document) |
### ISO 45001 — Occupational Health & Safety
Actions is used in EHS (Environment, Health & Safety) contexts where ISO 45001 compliance
is relevant. The platform supports these requirements through its action management capabilities.
| Clause | Requirement | Actions Implementation |
| 6.1.4 | Planning of actions for OH&S risks | Action creation with risk classification, priority, and regulatory deadlines |
| 8.2 | Emergency preparedness and response | Corrective action tracking with escalation rules and deadline enforcement |
| 9.1.2 | Evaluation of compliance | Compliance profile configuration, regulatory deadline tracking |
| 10.2 | Incident investigation and corrective action | Root cause analysis (RCA) schema, evidence attachment, consultation workflows |
| 5.4 | Worker participation and consultation | FR-CONSULT-REQUEST, FR-CONSULT-RESPOND, FR-CONSULT-CLOSE (Compliance Profile PRD) |
| 10.3 | Continual improvement | Action audit trails, state transition history, overdue monitoring |
## Breach Notification Process
The Actions platform must provide the data substrate to support GDPR Article 33/34
breach notification within 72 hours of discovery. This does not implement breach
detection (that is an operational/SIEM concern) — it ensures the system supports
breach investigation.
### Requirements
- Audit logs capture sufficient detail for forensic timeline reconstruction
- Action and evidence data is queryable by platform admins for impact assessment
- The compliance matrix documents the 72-hour notification obligation
- Append-only audit trail with database-level immutability provides tamper-evident audit trail for forensic analysis
The system provides the data substrate for breach investigation: affected tenant/action
counts, breach timeline from audit logs, and data categories exposed. All breach
investigation queries are restricted to the platform admin role, and audit log integrity
is enforced via append-only storage and ledger verification.
## Auditor Persona (P3)
For the full P3: Compliance Officer / Auditor persona definition, see
[Personas](../prd/personas.md).
